Title: Bursa Malaysia gains at opening on renewed buying in technology stocks
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Bursa Malaysia opened higher on Monday, tracking overnight gains on Wall Street as investors returned to technology stocks, which continue to benefit from the ongoing artificial intelligence (AI) supercycle.
At 9.10am, the benchmark FTSE Bursa Malaysia KLCI (FBM KLCI) rose 7.50 points to 1,691.13 from last week's close of 1,683.63.
The key index had opened 6.76 points up at 1,690.39.
Market breadth was positive, with gainers leading losers 349 to 160. A total of 273 counters were unchanged, 1,915 untraded and 36 suspended.
Turnover stood at 539.66 million shares worth RM217.18 million.
Malacca Securities Sdn Bhd said in a note it expected the rebound in the technology sector and continued to favour semiconductor companies as they remain among the biggest beneficiaries of the AI supercycle.
"We believe overall investor risk appetite should remain highly elevated as Elon Musk's SpaceX initial public offering makes its historic debut on Wall Street, closing at a US$2.1 trillion valuation on its first day of trading," it said.
Among heavyweights, Maybank and CIMB rose 16 sen to RM10.88 and RM7.55 respectively. Public Bank added four sen to RM4.87, Tenaga Nasional increased 10 sen to RM14.52 and IHH Healthcare was up three sen to RM8.81.
Among active stocks, Hong Seng Consolidated was flat at one sen, Tanco was down five sen to 15 sen and Top Glove slid one sen to 79.5 sen. ACE Market debutant Pentech gained five sen to 25 sen, while AirAsia X was six sen firmer at RM1.26.
Among top gainers, Malaysian Pacific Industries rose RM1.28 to RM48.78, Nestle increased RM1.20 to RM96.80, Batu Kawan added 50 sen to RM20.80, Vitrox inched up 23 sen to RM7.47 and Sunway Construction accumulated 17 sen to RM7.22.
Among top losers, Paragon fell 98 sen to RM2.29, Petronas Chemicals slid 47 sen to RM4.87,Ralco decreased 29 sen to 69 sen, Concrete Engineering lost 20 sen to RM4.00 and United Plantations was down 18 sen to RM31.32.
On the index board, the FBM Emas Index gained 63.95 points to 12,538.57, while the FBM Top 100 Index perked 61.56 points to 12,377.50 and the FBM Emas Shariah Index edged up 31.92 points to 12,454.22.
The FBM Mid 70 Index jumped 118.27 points to 18,002.42, while the FBM ACE Index accumulated 43.79 points to 4,712.46.
By sector, the Financial Services Index surged 186.34 points to 19,830.70 and the Plantation Index expanded 15.95 points to 8,768.03, while the Industrial Products and Services Index shed 2.85 points to 194.08 and the Energy Index declined 9.41 points to 765.96.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Rohingya say landowner built and offered illegal flats in Sungai Tekali
Article:
KAJANG: Rohingya refugees who previously rented illegal flats in Sungai Tekali, Hulu Langat, claimed that the landowner had offered them the housing units since 2019.
They claimed the landlord rented out nearly 30 housing units at rates between RM400 and RM750 a month, depending on size and facilities provided.
However, the Rohingya refugees began leaving the premises after a raid under Op Kutip carried out by the Immigration Department and the district police headquarters on Feb 28 last year.
One of them, Husain Abdullah, said the flats were built in stages in 2018 and were completed in 2019.
"The owner hired Indonesian and Vietnamese contract workers to build the flats at the front and back, resulting in about 25 to 30 housing units.
"Units were rented out at RM400, RM500, RM600 and RM750 to Rohingya residents, including myself, and the owner collected more than RM15,000 in monthly rental income.
"We were also provided with free electricity and water by the owner, and I believe it may have been taken illegally from nearby sources," he told Harian Metro.
He said residents in the surrounding area were aware of the existence of the flats occupied by foreigners, mainly from Myanmar, and knew the landowner.
"In 2014, the same landowner also built illegal housing next to the flats, but later demolished it after the current flats were completed," he said.
Another Rohingya refugee, Abdullah Saidurahman, rejected claims that the landowner was unaware that foreigners were living in the flats.
"There were two units rented by locals, while other units were rented by Rohingya residents.
"However, after the large-scale operation in February last year, all Rohingya residents moved out to other nearby locations.
"I want to clarify that it was the landowner himself who offered us the units, not subletting by other tenants," he said.
He said his intention in revealing the matter was to correct claims that Rohingya residents had been subletting the units.
Husain and Abdullah were among 42 people detained in the joint operation last year.
They said no Rohingya residents remained in the flats after the operation, following warnings from authorities not to stay there.
However, after the operation, one food outlet continued operating at the premises, run by Rohingya people.
The shop, located on the ground floor of the flats, is believed to serve alcoholic drinks to customers and Myanmar food.
A check yesterday afternoon found no demolition works being carried out on the illegal flats, unlike the previous day.
Since media exposure of the
issue
, the area has drawn public attention, especially from Hulu Langat residents who visited to see the site, which has also gone viral over the past week.
In addition, red spray paint was seen on the ground floor of the flats bearing the words "Ini Malaysia" and "Hantar Balik".
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: US, Iran reach agreement to end war, signing set for Friday
Article:
DUBAI/WASHINGTON: US and Iranian officials said on Sunday they had agreed on a framework to end their war, halt the US blockade of Iran and reopen the Strait of Hormuz, a preliminary pact that sent oil prices falling but leaves the fate of Iran's nuclear programme to further negotiations.
"The Deal with the Islamic Republic of Iran is now complete," US President Donald Trump wrote on his Truth Social platform around 5.30pm ET local time in Washington (2130 GMT) on Sunday. His post came shortly after Pakistani Prime Minister Shehbaz Sharif, whose country has served as a mediator, announced a deal had been struck early on Monday local time.
The memorandum of understanding is scheduled to be officially signed on Friday in Switzerland.
The precise terms were not immediately known. Sharif said in a post on X that the pact called for "the immediate and permanent termination of military operations on all fronts, including in Lebanon."
Lebanon has been a sticking point in negotiations, with Israel and Hizbollah ignoring calls from Trump and others to stop their attacks on each other in recent weeks.
In a statement, the secretariat of Iran's Supreme National Security Council said war and military operations on all fronts, including Lebanon, would end permanently starting on Monday night.
There was no immediate reaction to the announcement from Israel, which has said it was not party to the US-Iran talks.
[[nid:1463565]]
STRAIT TO REOPEN
Trump said the Strait of Hormuz, a major shipping route for global oil and gas supplies that Iran has effectively shut down for months, would open on Friday, and that he had ordered the end of the US blockade of Iranian ports.
"Ships of the World, start your engines. Let the oil flow!" Trump wrote.
Oil prices fell on the news. Brent crude futures fell 4 per cent in early trading on Monday, while US West Texas Intermediate slid more than 4.6 per cent. Stock markets in Asia jumped.
Former Biden administration State Department spokesperson Matthew Miller said Trump had made important concessions to Iran to achieve the status quo that existed before he launched the war.
"We have no assurances the nuclear programme will ever be addressed, but Iran has shown the world it can take the global economy hostage and get something from the US in return," said Miller.
Iran's deputy foreign minister, Kazem Gharibabadi, said a more expansive agreement would be negotiated during a 60-day ceasefire period, including sanctions relief for Iran.
The fate of Iran's nuclear programme, another thorny issue, will also be addressed in those later talks, sources previously told Reuters.
Thousands of people have been killed, mostly in Iran and Lebanon, since US and Israeli forces first attacked Iran on Feb 28. Iran has struck Israel and Gulf states hosting US bases and has effectively blockaded the Strait of Hormuz, pushing up global energy prices. US forces have blocked Iranian ports in response.
The Iran war has become a political liability at home for Trump and his fellow Republicans in Congress, with public opinion polls showing Americans deeply frustrated by rising gas prices ahead of November's midterm elections. But Trump has also faced pressure from members of his own party who insist that Iran's nuclear programme must be completely shut down.
Republican Senator Lindsey Graham, a leading Iran hawk, praised the deal but said he would be "watching closely" the coming negotiations on Iran's nuclear programme.
"Under our law, any nuclear deal with Iran will be sent to Congress for review and a vote," he said. "Congratulations to all in getting us to this point."
During his first term, Trump withdrew the US from a 2015 multilateral Iran deal, negotiated by Democratic President Barack Obama, that lifted sanctions on Teheran in exchange for limits on its nuclear programme, including international inspections.
Iran responded by ramping up its enrichment of uranium, producing more than 400 kg (around 900 pounds) of material at close to bomb-grade purity. The eventual fate of that uranium is likely to be a key negotiating point during the upcoming talks.
[[nid:1463564]]
TRUMP CALLS NETANYAHU 'A VERY DIFFICULT GUY'
The agreement was sealed despite an Israeli strike on Lebanon on Sunday that drew criticism from both Iran and Trump.
Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu has differed with Trump over American demands that Israel curb its military action in Lebanon to allow the United States to reach a deal with Iran.
Israel has said it will retain freedom of operations in Lebanon, while Iran has made a full ceasefire there an important component of its demands.
Trump updated Netanyahu on the progress towards a peace deal during a phone call on Sunday, Israel's N12 reported, citing a senior official.
In an interview with the New York Times, Trump called Netanyahu "a very difficult guy" and argued the Israeli leader should thank him for saving Israel from a nuclear-armed Iran.
Before the deal was announced, a senior Iranian official earlier told Reuters that, under the terms of the draft, the United States would agree to release US$25 billion of frozen Iranian assets.
A US official, also speaking before the announcement, said the agreement would ultimately lead to the dismantling of Iran's nuclear programme, with its stockpile of highly enriched uranium to be destroyed and removed.
A senior Iranian official said the draft deal would allow Iran, which denies seeking a nuclear bomb, to dilute its enriched uranium inside the country.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Perak floods: 92 evacuated to two relief centres
Article:
IPOH: The number of flood evacuees housed at temporary relief centres in Perak rose to 92 people from 33 families this morning, compared with 17 people from eight families last night.
The State Disaster Management Committee Secretariat said two relief centres had been opened in the two affected districts - Larut, Matang and Selama at 8pm, and Manjung at 11.30pm last night.
"The relief centre at SK Batu Hampar in the Larut, Matang and Selama district recorded an increase in evacuees compared with last night, housing 41 people from 14 families following floods affecting Kampung Batu 25, Kampung Berjaya, Kampung Batu 23 and Sungai Rotan.
"Meanwhile, the relief centre at SK Beruas in the Manjung district recorded 51 evacuees from 19 families from flood-affected areas in Kampung Kilang, Taman Anggerik Permai and Kampung Pulau Kabung," it said in a statement.
Meanwhile, the Malaysian Meteorological Department (MetMalaysia) issued a thunderstorm warning at 5.30am today for the areas of Kerian, Larut, Matang and Selama, Bagan Datuk, Manjung and Hilir Perak.
In addition, the Department of Irrigation and Drainage reported that water levels in Sungai Perak in the districts of Hilir Perak and Bagan Datuk had exceeded danger levels, with readings of 1.38m and 0.66m, respectively.
– Bernama
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Stepson of Norway's crown prince to hear verdict in rape trial
Article:
OSLO: The stepson of Norway's Crown Prince Haakon will learn on Monday whether he has been found guilty of charges of rape, domestic violence, assault and drug possession, among other crimes, in a case that has transfixed the Nordic country.
Marius Borg Hoiby, 29, who joined the royal family when his mother Mette-Marit married Haakon in 2001, pleaded not guilty to the most severe accusations against him, including those of rape, while admitting to some lesser charges.
If found guilty on all counts by the Oslo district court, he could be sentenced to seven years and seven months in prison, as requested by the prosecution, though the court can increase or decrease the length of the sentence sought.
The seven-week trial has gripped the country, detailing Hoiby's drug addiction, self-made videos of sexual encounters and more than 800 electronic messages entered into evidence. One alleged rape took place in the basement of the crown prince's family home, the court heard.
CASE HAS DENTED POPULARITY OF ROYALS
Interest in the case was boosted by the contrast between the picture-perfect royal family and Hoiby's alleged actions as heard in court, said Ketil Raknes, an associate professor in political communication at the Kristiania University of Applied Sciences.
The case, alongside other crises, has contributed to a decline in the popularity of the royal family.
It coincided with Crown Princess Mette-Marit's apology for "poor judgment" in maintaining contact with the late US sex offender Jeffrey Epstein after he was convicted in 2008.
A Norstat survey out on Feb 21 — during the trial — showed a fall in the number of Norwegians favouring keeping the monarchy to a record low of 60 per cent, from 70 per cent in January, and a rise to 27 per cent from 19 per cent in those wanting a different system of governance.
In May, the royal family recovered somewhat in popularity, with 64 per cent polled by Norstat supporting the monarchy and 23 per cent wanting a different system of governance.
"It was ... a perfect crisis for the royal family because they had two crises at the same time. And they had a lot of (criticism) for the way they handled the Epstein files," Raknes said.
MOTHER'S ILLNESS CHANGING VIEW
The verdict will be delivered amid difficult personal circumstances for Mette-Marit, Hoiby's mother, who this month was placed on the national lung transplant list as her health has severely deteriorated.
She suffers from pulmonary fibrosis, a condition that makes it increasingly difficult to breathe. Without a transplant, she has around a year left to live, her doctors have said.
That is somewhat changing people's perceptions ahead of the verdict, Raknes said.
"The coverage is much more sober," he said. "People are seeing: 'OK, this is a family who's really struggling and this is not the time for ... playing ... the moral card as high as we maybe have done earlier in this case'."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Oil prices down more than 4pct after US-Iran deal to open Hormuz
Article:
TOKYO: Oil prices plunged more than four per cent Monday after the United States and Iran announced they had reached a peace agreement to end months of a war that had roiled energy markets.
West Texas Intermediate was down 4.39 per cent at US$81.15 a barrel in Tokyo after mediator Pakistan said the United States and Iran agreed to a peace deal and an "immediate and permanent" end to military operations on all fronts, including Lebanon.
"The Deal with the Islamic Republic of Iran is now complete," US President Donald Trump swiftly confirmed with his own statement on Sunday.
Trump also announced that the strategic Strait of Hormuz -- a vital maritime chokepoint through which roughly 20 per cent of the world's crude oil supply transits -- would reopen.
"I hereby fully authorize the toll free opening of the Strait of Hormuz, and, simultaneously herewith, authorize the immediate removal of the United States Naval blockade. Ships of the World, start your engines. Let the oil flow!"
Soon after, Iran said that the newly announced agreement with the United States put an "immediate end" to the countries' war.
"This is a first step deal, not a final peace settlement," Stephen Innes of SPI Asset Management said.
"The market will now trade verification," he said including the official signing in Switzerland, mine clearance and Israeli restraint.
"It is a marketable ceasefire framework that kicks the hard problems down the road. Iranian compliance, and Hezbollah quiet."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: #NSTviral: Driver claims chased, car struck after brief honk
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: A road encounter went viral on Threads after a driver claimed she was chased and her car struck following a brief honk at a motorist said to have encroached into her lane.
In a post on
Threads, user @whoisfaanyway
said she honked briefly as a warning after the pickup truck driver failed to notice her vehicle beside him.
"I encountered a road bully today. I honked to alert him as he was coming into my lane. Then he chased me, cut me again and hit my car," she wrote.
In a video and photos shared online, the pickup truck driver was also seen allegedly making an obscene gesture when she overtook the vehicle.
The incident has sparked concern among social media users over road rage and reckless driving behaviour.
One user said: "Everyone is sensitive about being honked at, even when they are in the wrong. The horn is a safety device, not a trigger for anger."
Another user suggested that a police report be lodged, saying: "Showing an obscene gesture is an offence punishable by law. I suggest you lodge a report with video evidence."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Highland Towers: Final chapter as demolition looms
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Highland Towers tragedy, which claimed 48 lives on Dec 11, 1993, remains one of the worst building disasters in the country's history.
The incident occurred at about 1.35pm when Block 1 of the condominium collapsed following a slope failure due to continuous heavy rain.
Following the tragedy, residents of Block 2 and Block 3 were ordered to evacuate for safety reasons.
Since then, both blocks have remained unoccupied for more than three decades.
The disaster also triggered a prolonged legal dispute involving residents, developers and the Ampang Jaya Municipal Council (MPAJ).
On Dec 4, 1996, condominium owners filed a lawsuit against multiple parties, including MPAJ, seeking damages estimated at between RM31 million and RM35 million for the loss of their homes.
The compensation claims went through various stages of trial before being brought to the Federal Court.
In 2006, the Federal Court ruled that 73 residents of Block 2 and Block 3 failed in their claims against MPAJ, while efforts to demolish the remaining two blocks faced years of delays due to legal action and ownership complications.
After 32 years, the remaining two blocks of Highland Towers continue to stand as dilapidated, abandoned structures that still draw public attention.
The New Straits Times reported that the remaining blocks will finally be
demolished by the end of the year
after the magistrate's court approved an application filed by MPAJ last week.
MPAJ deputy president Hasrolnizam Shaari said the decision paves the way to tear down the long-abandoned structures.
"The coordination meeting involving unit owners, developers and insolvency authorities will ensure the process is completed within the year," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Improved market sentiment lifts ringgit at opening
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The ringgit extended its upward momentum, opening firmer on Monday against the US dollar and other major currencies, supported by improved risk sentiment following signs of easing geopolitical tensions.
At 8am, the local note rose to 4.0430/0500 against the greenback from last Friday's close of 4.0555/0600.
Bank Muamalat Malaysia Bhd chief economist Dr Mohd Afzanizam Abdul Rashid said news on the likely reopening of the Strait of Hormuz following the agreement between the United States and Iran on a peace deal to be signed on June 19 has boosted market sentiment.
"WTI and Brent crude oil prices have already fallen by more than four per cent, with Brent crude currently trading at US$83.83 per barrel.
"My sense is that the ringgit is moving towards the RM4.00 level against the US dollar. It may not happen immediately, but a sub-RM4.00 level would be consistent with the ringgit's fair value given the prevailing macroeconomic conditions and the resilience of Malaysia's economic growth," he told Bernama.
At the opening, the local currency strengthened against a basket of major currencies.
It appreciated versus the Japanese yen to 2.5275/5320 from 2.5334/5364 at last Friday's close, climbed against the British pound to  5.4374/4468 from 5.4429/4489 and improved vis-a-vis the euro to 4.6911/6992 from 4.6979/7031 previously.
The ringgit was also better against regional peers.
It gained versus the Singapore dollar to 3.1554/1611 from 3.1602/1640 last Friday and jumped against the Thai baht at 12.4015/4298 compared to 12.4105/4288 previously.
It also recovered vis-a-vis the Indonesian rupiah to 226.3/226.8 from 227.0/227.4 and was up versus the Philippine peso to 6.65/6.66 from 6.67/6.68 previously.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Gold gains over 1pct after US, Iran reach peace deal
Article:
NEW YORK: Gold rose more than one per cent on Monday, after US and Iran officials said they had reached a deal to end their conflict, pushing oil prices lower and easing concerns about inflation and higher interest rates.
Spot gold was up 1.8 per cent at US$4,297.42 per ounce, as of 0010 GMT, its highest level since June 9. US gold futures for August delivery rose 1.9 per cent to US$4,318.10.
US and Iranian officials said on Sunday they had agreed on a peace framework for a deal to end their war, halt the US blockade of Iran and reopen the Strait of Hormuz.
The pact will be officially signed on Friday in Switzerland, Pakistani Prime Minister Shehbaz Sharif said in a post on X.
Oil prices slipped more than four per cent and the US dollar fell to a 10-day low following the announcement.
Gold prices have been under pressure since the start of the US-Israeli war against Iran in late February. The effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz has led to a sharp increase in global oil prices, stoking inflation concerns and raising expectations of interest rates staying higher for longer.
Though traditionally seen as an inflation hedge, gold loses appeal in a high interest-rate environment as ⁠the opportunity cost of holding the non-yielding asset increases.
Markets see a 64 per cent chance of a US interest rate hike in December this year after the peace deal, down from 69 per cent last week, according to the CME FedWatch tool.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: MAIWP highlights holistic development at Darul Kifayah
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Students from Kompleks Darul Kifayah brought energy and enthusiasm to their annual sports carnival closing ceremony at Setiawangsa Mini Stadium here on Sunday.
The complex, a welfare and residential institution under the Federal Territory Islamic Religious Council (MAIWP), hosted 186 students across a range of sporting disciplines throughout the carnival.
The event opened with a marching procession by students dressed as popular food-and-beverage brands.
MAIWP Human Development Division senior deputy manager Damithah Iberahim, who was present at the event, said sports carnivals are held across all institutions under the council as part of its commitment to holistic human development.
"Through sports, students are able to uncover their talents.
"This carnival is also held as a closed event among Darul Kifayah students themselves to strengthen the bonds between them."
She said  many students at Darul Kifayah come from varied and challenging backgrounds, including those facing family problems, financial hardship and social issues, and that the institution plays a key role in reshaping their character through structured programmes.
"When they enter Darul Kifayah, the institution tries to mould their attitudes through programmes we organise every year.
[[nid:1463537]]
"We have allocated budgets for programmes aimed at developing students' minds, building their identity and uncovering their talents," she said.
For the students themselves, the carnival was as much about connection as it was competition.
Putri Syafiqah Adilah Anwar, a Form 5 student taking part in what she described as her final sports carnival at Darul Kifayah, said the event held special meaning for her.
"I enjoy this sports event because we get to strengthen our bonds
of friendship."
She added that the close-knit nature of the institution, with just over 100 students in total, made the connections formed all the more meaningful.
[[nid:1463536]]
The sentiment was echoed by Abir Taqiiyah Sollehin, who said students had spent the entire week leading up to the closing ceremony preparing for the track-and-field events, on top of months of court sports that began as far back as March.
"We  then continued after the Hari Raya Aidilfitri holidays," she said.
Darul Kifayah Sports Carnival Committee member Khairi Shah Mohd Ariffin commended the organising committee for executing the carnival with distinction, noting that the event reflected a broader commitment to developing students beyond the classroom.
"The success of this event proves the commitment of all parties in ensuring the holistic development of Darul Kifayah students, including in terms of discipline, health and mental well-being."
He said  the carnival, which began in February, drew participation from 186 students — 87 boys and 99 girls — across sports including volleyball, futsal, netball, sepak takraw, football and badminton, with the closing day dedicated to track and field events such as the 100m, 200m and 400m races.
The carnival also featured a house procession competition involving four sports houses — Al-Farabi, Al-Jundi, Al-Khawarizmi and Al-Ghazali — with Al-Khawarizmi emerging as overall champions.
Khairi Shah also praised the decision to hold the carnival at a stadium and encouraged Darul Kifayah to field teams in the Community Sports League under the Batu District Youth and Sports Department, noting that top performers could have the opportunity to represent the district at state level.
Adding to the festivities, teachers took part in a fun games segment, drawing laughter and cheers from students on the sidelines before the ceremony culminated in an awards presentation recognising top performers in running, long jump and high jump.
MAIWP has been mandated to intensify efforts to eradicate hardcore poverty across the Federal Territories, with Darul Kifayah serving as one of its key platforms for asnaf welfare and human development.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Oil prices tumble as US-Iran peace deal paves way for reopening of Strait of Hormuz
Article:
ISTANBUL: Oil prices fell sharply late on Sunday after an agreement was announced to end the conflict between the US and Iran by mediator Pakistan, Anadolu Ajansi reported.
Brent futures, which were trading above US$87, fell to below the US$84 level, down roughly 4 per cent day on day as of 2222 GMT following news of the planned agreement between Washington and Tehran, which is set to be signed on Friday after more than three months of conflict.
Pakistani Prime Minister Shehbaz Sharif announced early on Monday that the US and Iran had reached the agreement following intensive negotiations, with both sides declaring an immediate and permanent end to military operations across all fronts, including in Lebanon.
According to Sharif, the official signing ceremony is scheduled to take place on June 19 in Switzerland.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Markets cheer Iran deal, wait to see oil start flowing
Article:
NEW YORK: Stocks and bonds rallied and the dollar fell in Asia trade on Monday after the US and Iran agreed a peace deal to re-open the Strait of Hormuz and lift a US blockade on Iran.
US crude futures fell more than four per cent, S&P 500 futures rose about 0.8 per cent and the dollar was down broadly, lifting the yen to 159.7 per dollar and the euro to US$1.1616.
Here are market analysts' comments on the deal:
MRE SPEIZER, MARKET STRATEGIST, WESTPAC, AUCKLAND:
"It's positive for risk assets, positive for risky currencies, negative for the US dollar.
"There's still a few minor concerns - the Israeli skirmish with Lebanon remains a risk. And then you've got the signing date's Friday, and that's a long way off in this sort of environment. If it all goes according to plan...there'll probably be one more leg to this set of mini rallies.
"Inflation expectations might slip, but inflation itself is going to linger for some time. Because supply chains don't suddenly get fixed, and oil back on tap is not immediate."
BRIAN JACOBSEN, CHIEF ECONOMIC STRATEGIST, ANNEX WEALTH MANAGEMENT, WISCONSIN:
"For the Federal Reserve, this should make their life easier. Oil prices are falling, and gasoline prices should follow. Some will question whether this is a durable deal or not, but the Fed isn't supposed to set monetary policy based on political speculation. At their upcoming meeting, they'll likely remove their language about having an easing bias, but at least now they almost surely won't include language with a hiking bias."
JASON WONG, SENIOR MARKETS STRATEGIST, BNZ, WELLINGTON:
"This has been well anticipated, that's why I think the market reaction can be pretty well contained. What you see on your screens today - we're probably most of the way there now.
"It's a good sign, hopefully we can put this behind us and focus on macro-economic forces...the market will assume things will gradually return to normal. It's no longer a risk overhanging the market."
NICK TWIDALE, CHIEF MARKET STRATEGIST, ATFX GLOBAL, SYDNEY:
"I think we'll see the dollar fall over the course of the next few sessions. We'll probably see some of the risk currencies like Aussie and yen appreciate a little bit. But I don't think we're going to see any huge moves.
"There's going to be a lot of wait and see, on how quickly the Strait really reopens and how long it's going to take for oil flow to really get back to normal. It's certainly going to be months rather than weeks.
"I don't think we're going to see US$70 oil too quickly."
KRISTINA CLIFTON, SENIOR CURRENCY STRATEGIST, COMMONWEALTH BANK OF AUSTRALIA, SYDNEY
"It's obviously good news for the global economy that the Strait of Hormuz will reopen. It has been our view, though, that it's going to take some time for oil and gas flows to restart in full. Markets will be focused on how traffic is returning...and seeing how quickly production can come back online.
"It is our view that energy prices are not going to go back down to the levels that they were pre-conflict for quite some time...and it's going to take a while for traffic to go back to normal as well."
MAHJABEEN ZAMAN, HEAD OF FX RESEARCH ANZ, SYDNEY:
"This good news has been expected for some time now, and markets have been inching, waiting, with some of the positive vibe already embedded in pricing.
"Looking at cyclical FX, I think there is room for upside from where it is right now. You may see (oil) break US$80 on just, you know, happy days today...but then maybe the market will realise that, oh, wait a minute, maybe the terms of the deal may not be as lucrative. We also think that oil prices will remain a little bit on the higher side only because infrastructure has been damaged."
CHRIS WESTON, HEAD OF RESEARCH, PEPPERSTONE, MELBOURNE:
"It looks credible and it looks enough for the market to move on. We're looking now at what Hormuz looks like in terms of the ramp-up of cargo and logistics through the channel, given there have been some structural changes (and) damage to refineries.
"I think there's going to be a lot of other risk assets which are going to try to move on other factors such as the ramp-up of demand, people are looking at earnings again and central bank expectations this week.
"I think the trade is short volatility here. And that's going to allow risk to come on...a further decline in long-end bond yields would be certainly quite welcome for equity risk."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Income-based housing model proposed to replace RM300,000 cap
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Malaysia should move away from its RM300,000 affordable housing benchmark and adopt a more locally tailored affordability framework, similar to Singapore's Housing and Development Board (HDB) model, to better address structural mismatches in the property market, experts said.
They argue that the current price-based threshold is increasingly out of step with household income levels, financing capacity and regional cost variations, contributing to a build-up of unsold stock even in the so-called affordable segment.
Instead of a fixed price ceiling, Malaysia should adopt a more dynamic affordability formula that takes into account median income, debt servicing ratios and location-specific cost structures, similar to the Singapore model where public housing pricing is closely aligned with household income profiles and long-term financing frameworks.
Such an approach could help improve demand alignment, reduce property overhang and ensure that new housing supply better matches actual purchasing power across different income groups and regions.
The recommendation comes amid a persistent overhang in Malaysia's lower-priced housing segment, where a significant number of units priced below RM300,000 remain unsold, according to data from the National Property Information Centre (Napic).
Napic data showed that 14,201 completed residential units worth RM2.77 billion remained unsold as of the first quarter of this year. These accounted for 43.3 per cent of the country's total property overhang, with the bulk of the unsold units located in the Klang Valley, Johor and Perak.
Juwai IQI co-founder and group chief executive officer Kashif Ansari said the data points to a clear mismatch between housing supply and market demand.
More than half of all unsold homes have been on the market for at least six years, even as hundreds of thousands of homes continued to be purchased annually over the same period, he added.
"This indicates that the issue is less about overall affordability or financing and more about the specific characteristics, location, pricing, or appeal of certain properties.
"While discounts, financing support, or incentives may help improve buyer interest, long-unsold properties often face challenges tied to the homes themselves rather than a lack of willing buyers," Kashif told Business Times.
Universiti Teknologi Malaysia associate professor in property economics Dr Muhammad Najib Razali said homes priced below RM300,000 remain unsold because being "cheap by price band" does not necessarily mean they are "affordable by income capacity".
He said that while the unsold homes may be below the policy ceiling, it can still exceed the sustainable borrowing capacity of many Malaysian households.
This is particularly after factoring in transportation costs, maintenance expenses, down payment requirements and loan eligibility constraints.
"RM300,000 is only partly affordable for Malaysian buyers. While it falls within the government's "affordable housing" category, actual affordability depends on household income, financing capacity and living costs.
"Malaysia's median household income is about RM7,017 per month, or roughly RM84,000 annually.
"A widely used affordability benchmark suggests that a house should cost no more than about three times a household's annual income," he said.
Najib said that based on this measure, the affordable housing price for a median Malaysian household is about RM253,000, meaning a RM300,000 home exceeds the conventional affordability threshold by roughly RM47,000.
Following Singapore's Suit
Najib said Singapore's HDB model ties housing eligibility, grants and access to household income ceilings and buyer profiles, instead of relying on a single universal price benchmark.
He added that income ceilings are set according to project and household type, while Central Provident Fund housing grants are also adjusted based on income levels.
"Malaysia could adapt this by setting affordable prices at the district level; for example, housing costs should not exceed about 30 per cent of local household income after allowing for transport and maintenance," he said.
He also suggested that the government should use public land more strategically, noting that Singapore's relative success is not only driven by grants but also by state-led land assembly, long leasehold tenures, integrated town planning, and proximity to transport, schools and employment centres.
Najib said Malaysia could similarly leverage federal and state land near MRT, LRT, KTM and bus corridors for housing development while introducing resale restrictions, owner-occupation requirements and other anti-speculation measures.
Kashif said that to address unsold homes, proposals include improving liveability and connectivity to make the units more attractive to buyers, or converting them for alternative uses.
He said introducing mini-bus routes to affordable housing areas with weak public transport links allows residents to better access workplaces and schools.
"Probably more important is making sure that future affordable homes are built where the people who need them will buy them. This is also being done.
"The government is emphasising transit-orientated development, which means building homes near mass transit hubs. It's no good having an affordable home if you can't get from there to your job," he said.
Kashif said the industry believes the 30 per cent quota could be adjusted to place greater emphasis on housing data in guiding new supply, ensuring developments are aligned with actual local demand.
Revising Definition
Najib said the definition of "affordable housing" may need to be revised, noting that a single RM300,000 benchmark is analytically weak because income levels, cost of living, transport dependency and employment opportunities vary significantly across different regions.
He added that a price considered affordable in one area may be financially unrealistic in another, such as between Kuala Lumpur, Selangor, Kelantan, Sabah and secondary towns.
"The current approach assumes that affordability is determined mainly by the selling price. In reality, affordability depends on whether households can still maintain a reasonable standard of living after paying for housing," he said.
Najib said affordable housing should be determined based on the income households have left after covering essential expenses such as food, transport, childcare, utilities and existing debts.
He added that this approach could be simplified into a practical policy framework, where housing affordability is adjusted according to local economic conditions rather than a single nationwide price ceiling.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: From classroom to chaos: Inside Malaysia's PR internship reality
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: In lecture halls, communication feels orderly. Models align, frameworks hold, and case studies resolve neatly on the final slide.
Step outside, and that clarity dissolves.
For students of public relations and strategic communication, internships are not a procedural requirement. They are the first real encounter with consequence. Words are no longer graded; they are published, questioned, and sometimes challenged in real time.
This is where Malaysia's future communicators begin to take shape.
When theory meets pressure
Interns entering agencies, corporations, government bodies, or media organisations quickly realise they are not observers. They are contributors.
They draft media statements, prepare social media content, update briefing notes, and respond to fast-moving requests. The pace is immediate, and the margin for error is thin.
A delayed reply can mean a missed media opportunity. A poorly phrased sentence can distort a message.
What classrooms frame as "best practice" becomes, in reality, a balancing act between speed, accuracy, and judgement.
What cannot be taught in class
Internships teach dimensions of communication that no textbook can fully replicate.
Students develop instinct, knowing when to speak, when to hold back, and when to question. They learn context, how the same message can be interpreted differently by stakeholders with competing interests. Most importantly, they experience consequences, how minor oversights can escalate into public issues.
Under pressure, theory becomes fluid. Deadlines shift, information evolves, and decisions must still be made.
For many, the turning point comes quietly: seeing their work published, used in a campaign, or delivered in a real briefing. That moment reframes confidence not as academic performance but as practical capability.
Discovering fit and direction
Internships also answer a more personal question: is this the right field?
The perception of public relations as polished and event-driven often gives way to a more demanding reality—long hours, constant coordination, and the need to manage uncertainty.
Some students thrive in this environment. Others realise their strengths lie elsewhere.
Both outcomes matter. Internships serve as a testing ground, helping students refine their career direction early, whether in agencies, in-house roles, public service, or adjacent fields.
Uneven experiences
Not all internships offer equal value.
Some organisations provide structured exposure, mentorship, and meaningful work. Interns participate in meetings, receive feedback, and understand how their tasks contribute to broader strategies.
Others offer little more than routine administrative duties, with minimal guidance or learning.
This disparity creates an uneven starting point. Two graduates may complete the same requirement, yet leave with vastly different levels of preparedness.
It raises a broader question about how seriously internships are treated within the talent pipeline.
The role of lecturers and universities
This is where academic oversight becomes critical.
In many programmes, lecturers conduct site visits during the internship period, engaging directly with workplace supervisors. These sessions provide insight into student performance, workplace expectations, and the alignment between academic training and industry needs.
The feedback gathered is not merely administrative. Lecturers compile reports that are reviewed at the departmental level, where recurring patterns, both strengths and gaps, are identified.
When consistent feedback emerges across multiple organisations, universities can recalibrate course content, ensuring that what is taught remains relevant to industry realities.
This loop between classroom and workplace strengthens the overall quality of training.
At the same time, organisations themselves are evaluated. Those that consistently fail to provide proper supervision or learning opportunities may be excluded from future placements. Conversely, organisations that demonstrate strong mentorship and structured programmes become preferred partners.
This informal "blacklisting" and "gold-listing" mechanism helps safeguard the integrity of internships while rewarding employers who invest in talent development.
Drawing the line on exploitation
There is, however, a persistent concern that cannot be ignored.
Interns, eager to prove themselves, are often placed in positions where expectations exceed reasonable learning boundaries. Long hours, minimal compensation, and unclear roles can shift the experience from developmental to extractive. Again, it should be beyond photocopying, PowerPoint slides, binding or making refreshments.
The justification is often framed as "industry reality". But exposure should not come at the cost of basic fairness.
The distinction is simple: an internship should prioritise learning alongside contribution. When the balance tilts too far toward output with little guidance, its purpose is undermined.
Feedback as a missing link
Another common gap is the absence of meaningful feedback.
Many interns complete their attachment with little more than a generic evaluation. Without specific guidance, it is difficult to understand what improved and what did not.
Constructive feedback, clear, direct, and actionable, is essential. It helps students refine their skills, adjust their approach, and build professional awareness.
Likewise, students must take initiative. Asking questions, seeking clarification, and reflecting on daily tasks turn routine work into active learning.
What employers actually value
Employers rarely prioritise perfect theory.
Instead, they look for individuals who can write clearly, adapt quickly, and respond responsibly. Curiosity, reliability, and awareness often matter more than technical precision.
Internships provide a low-risk environment for employers to assess these qualities. For students, they function as an extended evaluation, one that often determines future opportunities.
Many full-time roles are secured not through interviews, but through consistent performance during internship periods. This is a wonderful window of opportunity; seize it.
A shared responsibility
For internships to remain meaningful, responsibility must be shared.
Universities must actively monitor placements and integrate industry feedback into curriculum design.
Employers must provide structure, mentorship, and fair treatment, recognising interns as potential future colleagues rather than temporary support.
Students must approach internships with intent—prepared, engaged, and accountable for their own development.
Why does it matter?
Internships represent more than a transition from campus to career. They are where theory is tested, habits are formed, and professional identities begin to emerge.
At their best, they transform students from passive learners into active communicators, individuals who understand that every message carries weight.
In a field where words can build or break trust, understanding this is nonnegotiable; it is the baseline. Not everyone gets a front-row seat inside a communications team, but if you do, treat it like a once-in-a-career opening. Do not blend into the background. Make yourself impossible to ignore by listening sharply, taking instructions seriously, and executing with speed and precision. Plenty of interns pass through unnoticed; your job is to be the one they remember when they ask, "Who's ready for the next hire?"
------------------------------------------------
The writer is an accredited PR practitioner and a fellow of the Institute of Public Relations Malaysia
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Two maids abused by employers found safe under Indonesian consulate protection
Article:
JOHOR BARU: The two maids seen abused by their employers in a viral video recently are currently under the protection of the Indonesian consulate-general here.
A consulate-general spokesman said both the women, identified only as YY and SH, have been placed in temporary housing pending further assistance.
The spokesman said the consular officers were informed of the case on Saturday by YY.
She had told the officers of the abuse she had suffered and two other Indonesian women, SH and YA, who worked as domestic helpers in Johor.
The spokesman said YY claimed all three of them were frequently abused by their employers.
The abuse, she had said, began towards the end of last year and lasted till January.
"Later, the trio were abandoned in around the Kampung Melayu Majidee area in Johor.
"As they wanted to continue working in Malaysia, they parted ways. YA headed to Kuala Lumpur, while YY and SH stayed on in Johor," the spokesman said in a statement today.
The statement added that all three were working in Malaysia illegally.
It said all their passports were kept by the employer, adding that this was why they did not report the abuse to the authorities.
The spokesman said YY eventually decided to report what happened to the consulate-general.
"After receiving the report, the consulate-general in Johor Baru immediately filed a complaint and began working with police.
"Based on the information received, Johor Baru Utara police detained four people on June 13.
"Besides placing YY and SH in temporary housing under our care, the consulate-general is also working with our embassy in Kuala Lumpur to trace the whereabouts of YA.
"This is so that she can also be placed under protection and given assistance," said the spokesman.
The consulate-general will provide assistance to all three woman in lodging police reports and provide them with legal advice.
It was reported yesterday that two sisters and their husbands, all of whom live together, were detained by police to facilitate investigations into the
abuse of their domestic helpers
in their home in Taman Johor, Tampoi.
The four, aged between 30 and 34, were detained about 7.30pm on Saturday.
A video clip of them assaulting two of the domestic helpers, secretly taken by the third maid, went viral.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Asean can lead the shift towards a more balanced global financial order
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: No country in the world should have the privilege of running persistent deficits because the rest of the world depends on its currency.
Yet this is arguably one of the unique advantages enjoyed by the US through the dominance of the US dollar in global trade finance and foreign exchange reserves.
Unlike most other currencies, whose strength is largely derived from economic fundamentals such as productivity, competitiveness and fiscal discipline, the US dollar also benefits from its entrenched position in the global financial system. This is reinforced by the fact that governments, corporations and central banks across the world continue to use the dollar as the primary medium for trade, investment and reserves. This creates a constant demand for dollars and US treasury securities, enabling Washington to finance deficits on a scale that would be difficult for most nations.
The world should gradually move towards a system where currencies derive their strength primarily from the intrinsic fundamentals of the economies that issue them rather than from an entrenched position within the international monetary system. Such a transition would create a more balanced, resilient and equitable global financial order.
The question is where such a process should begin. Asean is uniquely positioned to start the ball rolling.
The 10 Asean countries represent one of the world's most dynamic economic regions, yet much of the trade between them continues to be conducted using the US dollar, even when neither the buyer nor the seller is American. Businesses often convert local currencies into dollars before conducting transactions, creating unnecessary costs and reinforcing dependence on a currency external to the region.
The practical first step would be to increase the use of local currencies in intra-Asean trade. Malaysian companies trading with Indonesia, Thailand, Vietnam, Singapore and the Philippines should increasingly be able to settle transactions directly in their respective currencies. Such arrangements would reduce transaction costs, minimise exposure to exchange-rate volatility and strengthen regional financial cooperation.
Alongside this, Asean central banks could expand currency swap arrangements, improve regional payment infrastructure, and facilitate cross-border settlements using local currencies. These measures would gradually reduce the need for dollar intermediation without disrupting trade or financial stability.
Malaysia is well placed to champion this initiative as it maintains strong diplomatic relations with both Eastern and Western powers while pursuing an independent and pragmatic foreign policy. Once a stronger Asean mechanism is in place, this could be extended to other major economies such as China, India, Japan and South Korea. Together, these countries account for significant share of global trade and economic output. Increased use of regional currencies would reduce the excessive reliance on a single currency.
The next stage would involve expanding such arrangements to willing partners in the Middle East, Africa, and Latin America. Over time, a network of local-currency settlement mechanisms could emerge, giving nations greater flexibility in conducting trade and managing reserves.
This is not a call for the replacement of the US dollar. The dollar will likely remain a major international currency for many years to come. Rather, it is a call for the gradual evolution towards a multi-currency international financial system where currencies compete on the strength of the underlying economies.
Under such circumstances, the value of the US dollar would depend more directly on US economic fundamentals, fiscal discipline, innovation and institutional strength. Likewise, currencies around the world would rise and fall based on the performance of the economies behind them.
As economic power becomes increasingly dispersed across Asia and other emerging regions, the international financial system must adapt to reflect this reality. Asean has the economic weight, strategic location and collective interest to initiate the change.
The journey towards a more balanced financial order will not happen overnight. It will require patience, cooperation and political will. Asean can take that step and help shape a fairer and more resilient financial future.
-------------------------------------------
The writer holds an MBA from the University of Strathclyde in the UK, awarded through the prestigious Chevening Scholarship. With extensive experience in financial markets and a strong background in management education, he has also served in a prominent think tank. The opinions expressed are his own and do not necessarily represent the views of Business Times.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Who governs the chokepoints?
Article:
Federalism, Strategic Minerals and the New International Political Economy
KUALA LUMPUR: The political debates surrounding oil, gas, rare earths and critical minerals are often presented as disputes over ownership, revenue or jurisdiction. Yet history suggests that resource disputes are rarely about resources alone. More often, they are manifestations of deeper questions concerning power, representation, legitimacy and the relationship between different levels of government.
This reality is becoming increasingly important as the world enters an era defined not only by geopolitical competition but also by strategic competition over supply chains, technology and critical resources. According to the International Energy Agency, demand for critical minerals required for clean energy technologies is expected to grow significantly over the coming decades, driven by the expansion of electric vehicles, renewable energy systems, battery storage and advanced industrial technologies. What was once viewed primarily through the lens of economics is increasingly being viewed through the lens of national strategy.
For much of the twentieth century, oil occupied the centre of global economic and political calculations. Today, however, a broader range of strategic assets is shaping international affairs. Rare earth elements, lithium, nickel, cobalt, semiconductors, advanced materials, data infrastructure and artificial intelligence ecosystems have emerged as critical components of national power. These assets are no longer viewed simply as commercial commodities. They are increasingly regarded as instruments of statecraft.
Malaysia itself sits at the intersection of these trends. Beyond petroleum and natural gas, the country possesses growing potential in rare earths, advanced materials and semiconductor-related industries. At a time when governments around the world are seeking to diversify supply chains and reduce strategic vulnerabilities, the governance of these resources is becoming increasingly important not only for economic development but also for national resilience and international competitiveness.
The Rise of Chokepoint Economics
One of the defining characteristics of the contemporary international political economy is the growing importance of chokepoints.
Traditionally, chokepoints referred to narrow maritime passages such as the Strait of Malacca, the Suez Canal or the Panama Canal. Control over these routes provided strategic influence because they connected global trade flows and global commerce depended upon them.
Today, however, chokepoints extend far beyond geography. They increasingly exist within technology, finance and industrial supply chains.
A country may possess substantial mineral reserves, but if another country dominates the processing, refining or manufacturing stages of the value chain, the latter often holds greater strategic leverage. This explains why policymakers around the world have become intensely focused not merely on resource extraction but on the broader ecosystems surrounding strategic industries.
Recent restrictions on semiconductor technologies, debates over rare earth export controls, and growing competition over critical mineral supply chains demonstrate how economic interdependence is increasingly being used as an instrument of strategic influence. The modern contest is therefore not simply about who owns resources. It is about who controls the networks, technologies, standards and industrial capabilities that transform resources into strategic power.
In this environment, economic policy and national security have become increasingly intertwined.
Federalism in an Age of Strategic Competition
These developments carry profound implications for federations.
Classical political science distinguishes between unitary states, federations and confederations according to how sovereignty is distributed. In a federation, constitutional authority is shared between the national government and constituent states or provinces. The arrangement seeks to balance unity with diversity and national coherence with regional autonomy.
Yet strategic resources introduce a new dimension to this balance.
A state government may reasonably argue that resources located within its territory should generate tangible benefits for its citizens. Such arguments often reflect legitimate concerns regarding development, participation and local empowerment.
At the same time, federal governments increasingly view strategic resources through a national lens. Rare earths, energy assets, semiconductor facilities and critical mineral deposits can influence foreign policy, trade negotiations, defence planning and economic resilience. Their significance extends beyond the boundaries of any single state.
As a result, modern federations are confronted with a delicate challenge. They must preserve regional legitimacy while ensuring national strategic coherence.
This is not a uniquely Malaysian issue.
Canada continues to navigate tensions involving Alberta and energy policy. Australia has long balanced federal and state interests regarding mineral wealth. The United Kingdom has faced similar questions through Scotland and North Sea hydrocarbons. Across the world, federations are discovering that strategic resources test not merely economic arrangements but the broader political compact upon which national unity rests.
The Real Question Is Statecraft.
The deeper lesson is that resource disputes are rarely resolved through economics alone.
Throughout history, questions involving taxation, resource allocation and economic governance have often reflected underlying debates about political representation and legitimacy. The Boston Tea Party of 1773 was not fundamentally about tea. It was about who possessed the authority to make decisions and under what conditions that authority would be accepted as legitimate.
The same principle remains relevant today.
Whether the issue concerns petroleum, rare earths, strategic minerals or advanced technology industries, the central question is not simply who owns the resource. Rather, it is how competing interests can be reconciled within a framework that preserves both national cohesion and public trust.
This is where statecraft becomes essential.
Good statecraft requires governments to recognise that economic efficiency, political legitimacy and strategic security are not mutually exclusive objectives. Sustainable solutions emerge when institutions are capable of balancing these considerations simultaneously.
In an era of increasing geopolitical fragmentation, this balance is becoming more important than ever.
The Future Belongs to Strategic Federalism
The emerging challenge for many countries is therefore not whether to centralise or decentralise power. Such binary debates often obscure the real issue.
The more important question is how federations can develop governance arrangements that allow meaningful participation by states while maintaining the strategic coordination necessary for a competitive and uncertain world.
One possible answer lies in what might be described as strategic federalism.
Under such an approach, strategic resources remain embedded within a national framework, while states enjoy genuine participation in decision-making processes and equitable access to economic benefits. National interests and regional aspirations are not treated as opposing forces but as complementary components of a larger constitutional settlement.
As global competition increasingly revolves around critical minerals, advanced technologies and supply-chain resilience, the strength of a federation will depend not only on the resources it possesses but also on the institutions through which those resources are governed.
Ultimately, strategic resources may shape the future, but institutions will determine whether those resources become a source of national strength or political division.
In the twenty-first century, the future international political economy will be shaped less by geology than by governance.
------------------------------------------------
The writer is an adjunct lecturer at Universiti Teknologi Petronas and senior consultant with Global Asia Consulting. The opinions expressed are his own.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Malaysian homes get smarter
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Malaysia is emerging as a leading smart living market in Southeast Asia, supported by expanding digital infrastructure and growing public-private collaboration.
Samsung Malaysia Electronics president Charles Kim said that as connected devices become more widespread, privacy and cybersecurity remain key considerations.
He said the industry is shifting from safeguarding individual devices to protecting entire connected environments through integrated security frameworks.
"By combining robust security design with user awareness, smart homes can remain innovative and secure."
"Malaysia is focusing on practical, everyday value. Consumers prefer technologies that integrate seamlessly into their daily lives."
He said Malaysia is contributing to the region's smart living ecosystem.
Kim said smart home adoption in Southeast Asia is being driven by a combination of evolving consumer behaviour and maturing technology.
Increasingly, consumers view smart homes as practical lifestyle solutions rather than luxury add-ons.
This shift is reinforced by government digitalisation and sustainability initiatives.
He added that technology itself is no longer the primary barrier to adoption, as connected ecosystems and AI-enabled devices have become more intuitive and accessible.
While early market penetration in Malaysia was concentrated in urban and higher-income segments such as the Klang Valley, Kim said adoption is now expanding across the country.
"Strong Internet penetration, improving connectivity and a growing base of tech-savvy, value-conscious consumers are driving this shift.
"Adoption is visible across key categories such as entertainment, home appliances and climate control."
On regulation, Kim said Malaysia's digital and data governance frameworks are improving, but collaboration between government, industry and consumers remains essential as AI and Internet of Things (IoT) technologies evolve.
"At the same time, consumers also need to stay informed and take an active role in managing their data," he said.
Kim expects the next phase of smart living in Malaysia to be defined by intelligence, adaptability and seamless integration.
"For consumers, this means shifting from owning devices to embracing connected experiences while being mindful of data and security.
"For businesses, it requires moving towards ecosystem-driven models, prioritising interoperability and designing with privacy in mind."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Three Czechs die in catamaran, sailboat collision off Croatia
Article:
ZAGREB: Three Czechs were killed and one was missing after a large passenger catamaran carrying more than 100 people and a sailboat collided in Croatia's central Adriatic on Sunday, officials said.
"We were struck by the incredibly sad news of the death of at least three Czech citizens after a collision between a yacht and a catamaran in Croatia," a Czech foreign ministry spokesman told AFP.
The Czech consul is on site, spokesman Adam Corgo said, offering condolences to the victims' families.
The accident occurred in the waters between the central islands of Solta and Brac when a "passenger vessel operated by a private domestic company collided with a French-flagged sailboat, which then sank", the Croatian sea and transport ministry said earlier.
There were 118 passengers and seven crew members on the passenger vessel, and eight people on the sailboat, three of whom were killed, the statement added.
One person was still missing while the remaining four were rescued from the sea.
Croatian Prime Minister Andrej Plenkovic voiced condolences to the victims' families and his Czech counterpart Andrej Babis.
"An intensive search for one person is still ongoing," he said on X.
A Croatian navy underwater drone located the sunken sailboat at a depth of more than 50 metres (164 feet) and the missing person was believed to still be inside the vessel, Split harbourmaster Zeljko Kustera told the state-run broadcaster HRT.
The sailboat recovery operation will start on Monday morning, he said, calling the accident a "huge tragedy".
According to the Split hospital, three injured Czech nationals sustained minor injuries, while one remained hospitalised with serious spinal injuries.
No one aboard the catamaran was injured.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: [UPDATED] Trailer driver in fatal Karak Highway crash tests positive for drugs
Article:
BENTONG: The trailer driver involved in a fatal crash that killed four people at Km43 Kuala Lumpur-Karak Expressway yesterday has tested positive for drugs.
District police chief Superintendent Zaiham Mohd Kahar said urine screening confirmed that the suspect had consumed methamphetamine.
He said the driver was brought to the Bentong police headquarters after undergoing treatment at Bentong Hospital.
"Police conducted a urine test on the driver and he tested positive for methamphetamine. He was arrested about 4am today.
"Police will apply for a remand order against the driver at the Raub court to facilitate investigations," he said in a statement today.
Yesterday, Pahang police chief Datuk Seri Yahaya Othman said checks revealed that the lorry driver had
15 traffic summonses
.
The 4.25pm crash yesterday claimed the lives of four people, including an infant, after the car they were travelling in was crushed by the overturned trailer.
The victims — a man, two women and a year-old child — were
killed on the spot
, while the lorry driver sustained head and hip injuries.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Shah Alam cops probe fight between two students at football field
Article:
SHAH ALAM: Police are investigating a fight involving two school students at a mosque football field in Section 19 here last Friday.
District police chief assistant commissioner Sarudin Samah said police received a report from the father of one of the students at about 1pm.
He said initial investigations found that both male students, aged 13 and 14, knew each other and were from the same school.
"The altercation involved only the two students but is believed to have been witnessed by several other students at the scene.
"One of the students involved in the altercation sought treatment at Shah Alam Hospital and was confirmed to have sustained soft tissue injuries," he said in a statement today.
He said the case is being investigated under Section 323 of the Penal Code for voluntarily causing hurt.
The offence carries a maximum penalty of one year's jail and RM2,000 fine.
Those with information on the incident can contact the investigating officer Inspector Mohammad Amirul Zulkifli at 012-558 3436 or the nearest police station.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: G7 protest turns from carnival to violent stand-off
Article:
GENEVA: An anti-G7 protest in Geneva began in a carnival atmosphere on Sunday before descending into violence, with building facades smashed in and police firing tear gas at masked youths.
The day started in tranquil fashion: ahead of the march, people gathered in a park overlooking Lake Geneva, seeking shade from the blazing afternoon sun and catching up with friends as they arrived.
The atmosphere was festive, with people singing and dancing and putting the final touches to their placards, bearing slogans like "G7 = racket. Make anti-imperialism great again."
Marco, 36, who lives in Geneva, was among those in positive spirits.
"In a time when everyone's reading depressing news on their phones, it's an opportunity to see that the left is still alive – and maybe even making a resurgence," he told AFP.
The "No-G7" umbrella coalition, representing various causes, called the demonstration in the Swiss city to protest against the Group of Seven summit taking place in nearby Evian in France.
"It's been a long time since I protested," said Claude, a 65-year-old retiree from La Chaux-de-Fonds in northwest Switzerland. "Now there is an urgent need to protest."
He was on the march "to demonstrate against the assassins of humanity", he added.
[[nid:1463462]]
CAR ON FIRE, BUILDINGS ATTACKED
Several bands also took part, some with brass instruments, some playing Latin rhythms, people chanting slogans and dancing along as the demonstration followed a long loop through the northern half the city.
Many shop fronts along the route were boarded up, with the plywood then sprayed with graffiti.
Geneva's police said around 20,000 people attended; the vast majority were well behaved and not involved in any violence.
But merging in along the route were sections of masked, hooded invidividuals, many dressed all in in black. The police said around 600 so-called "Black Bloc" hardcore demonstrators had joined in the throng.
Not long into the demonstration, a Tesla car went up in flames.
A few firecrackers were thrown towards the riot police squads, many of whom were drafted in from other parts of Switzerland.
Demonstrators lit flares and the march came to a halt as the Geneva offices of the professional services firm PricewaterhouseCoopers came under sustained attack. There were repeated attempts to stave in the windows, the metal shutters were ripped off and the glass battered.
A red-and-white barricade was used to try to smash in the security cameras in the building's porch.
The police swiftly responded with tear gas, sending people running back into oncoming protesters, ducking through hedges into private gardens or seeking shelter in side alleys to rinse out their eyes.
[[nid:1463461]]
LENGTHY STAND-OFF
Some along the route had slapped stickers on street signs, saying refugees were welcome.
Later on, several glass panels surrounding the memorial to staff from the United Nations' refugee agency UNHCR who lost their lives while serving refugees were also damaged and sprayed in graffiti. Rubble littered the street.
The nearby International Telecommunication Union, the UN's telecoms agency, had its front doors smashed up.
As the march returned to the park where it began, tram stops were shattered, and flares were hurled towards the police, who responded with several rounds of tear gas.
A bin was set on fire in the road as the stand-off dragged on.
Police kept the demonstrators in the park, with tired-looking protesters let out in dribs and drabs as the light began to fade.
The Geneva cantonal police said they had made "several arrests", adding after sunset that "no injuries have been reported so far."
"As for property damage, it remains limited considering the significant number of disruptive elements identified," it added.
As the protest wound down, the No-G7 coalition said that "the spirit of Geneva was on our side – the spirit of freedom and human rights.
"We were able to send a message of solidarity and hope to the world."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: US to release US$12bil in frozen assets
Article:
TEHERAN: The United States will release US$12 billion in frozen assets to Iran before the start of negotiations, Mehr news agency reported on Monday, quoting a 14-point memorandum of understanding between the two nations.
The document published by Mehr stipulated "the release of US$24 billion in frozen Iranian assets during the 60-day negotiation period" that begins after the memorandum of understanding is concluded.
"Half of this amount must be made available to Iran before the start of the negotiations," the text, which has not been officially confirmed, specifies.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Iran military says it 'humiliated' US, Israel in war
Article:
TEHERAN: Iran's military said on Monday it had "humiliated" the United States and Israel, in a statement issued after an agreement to immediately end the conflict was announced.
Iranian forces "have, through the imposition of their divine and iron will upon the humiliated American and Zionist enemies, demonstrated with strength that the enemy has no path other than accepting defeat and surrender", the general staff said in a statement broadcast by state television.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Malaysia targets landfill reliance cut as food waste crisis deepens [WATCH]
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Malaysia is pivoting towards a more sustainable future by slashing the country's reliance on landfills from 61 per cent to 52 per cent by 2030.
The Solid Waste Management and Public Cleansing Corporation (SWCorp) said that under the 13th Malaysia Plan (2030 blueprint), the government will shift its focus from traditional disposal methods to a circular economy model, prioritising recycling, composting and advanced waste-to-energy technologies.
The agency said a vital part of this strategy is the reduction of food waste.
"Food waste makes up 40.03 per cent of the 36,900 tonnes of solid waste generated daily in the country.
"However, recent data from the EPIK (an online portal managed by SWCorp for premises to report their waste management practices) suggests that efforts to divert this waste are gaining momentum," the agency said.
SWCorp said that in 2025, licensed collection companies sent 7,125.77 tonnes of food waste for treatment, a significant jump from the 4,536 tonnes in 2024.
"The upward trajectory reflects a positive shift in compliance and the adoption of source separation practices, particularly among commercial, industrial and institutional (CII) premises.
"While food waste currently constitutes 37.04 per cent of total waste collected by SWCorp, it has increased its diversion target from four tonnes to seven tonnes to alleviate the pressure on the nation's 141 landfills."
The Landfill Crisis
SWCorp said of the 141 landfills currently in use, only 21 per cent (29 sites) are engineered sanitary landfills equipped with leachate treatment and gas venting systems.
"With traditional landfills reaching maximum capacity, the government is looking to the Low Carbon Cities Framework to guide the expansion of organic waste infrastructure, including biogas processing and composting facilities for agricultural use."
War on Food Waste
The agency is also doubling down on behavioural change through its flagship Hargai Makanan, Elak Pembaziran (Appreciate Food, Avoid Waste) campaign.
"The 2026 Ramadan served as a critical litmus test for these efforts.
"MySaveFood @ Ramadan programme rescued 72,000kg of surplus food nationwide, which is an increase from the 69,000kg saved the previous year.
"In collaboration with Yayasan Food Bank Malaysia and Pertubuhan Gema Malaysia (GEMA), the rescued food was distributed to B40 communities, welfare centres and university students," the agency said.
Policy and Enforcement
SWCorp currently enforces the Solid Waste and Public Cleansing Management Act 2007 across seven regions, including Kuala Lumpur, Johor and Kedah.
"While the separation of food waste in the CII sector is currently voluntary, SWCorp has signalled its intention to make food waste segregation mandatory in the near future.
"Handling food waste remains our most pressing logistical challenge. As the population grows, transitioning to circular technologies is no longer an option, but a necessity for urban sustainability," the agency added.
[[nid:1463453]]
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Demand for food aid jumps 40pct as living costs bite [WATCH]
Article:
RISING living costs have triggered a surge in demand for food aid, with The Lost Food Project (TLFP) reporting a 35 per cent to 40 per cent increase in the number of families seeking help between 2025 and 2026.
TLFP provides food aid for more than 36,000 B40 families across 180 locations, including people's housing projects (PPRs), civil society groups and various charities. This amounts to over 150,000 meals each week.
While the bulk of its operations is in the Klang Valley, the organisation also operates in Penang and Johor.
TLFP general manager Stuart Galvin said demand from low-income communities has continued to rise sharply.
"In early 2024, we reported a 30 per cent increase in demand. Through 2025 and into 2026, that trend has only accelerated," he told the NST.
"We estimate demand has grown by approximately 35 to 40 per cent over the past two years.
"Several factors are driving this, primarily the rising cost of living, which continues to squeeze B40 households who already spend over a third of their income on food."
TLFP is now in its 11th year of operations. In 2025, the organisation rescued 2.9 million kilogrammes of food from rotting in landfills, converting potential environmental hazards into 8.5 million meals for needy communities.
